message.content:
Below is a **tester-oriented mapping** of the **NIST AI RMF 1.0** Core functions and the **NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile** to concrete LLM/AI-app tests. I focus on controls that are *testable* in practice and that NIST explicitly ties to risk identification, measurement, or management. The **AI RMF 1.0** publication number is **NIST AI 100-1**[7], and the **Generative AI Profile** is **NIST AI 600-1**[4].

## 1) **GOVERN** — organization-level controls that can be tested as evidence, process, and traceability

- **Accountability / transparency** — check whether the organization has documented roles, escalation paths, approval gates, model ownership, and audit trails for model changes and incidents[7].
- **Privacy** — verify whether governance requires privacy review, data-handling approvals, retention limits, and incident handling for sensitive data used in training or prompts[7].
- **Security / resilience** — check whether AI security requirements, red-team signoff, incident response, and third-party dependency reviews are defined and enforced[7].
- **Fairness / bias** — verify whether governance requires bias review criteria, protected-class impact review, and signoff before deployment[7].
- **What a tester would check** — existence and completeness of policies, evidence of enforcement, change-management records, incident tickets, model cards/system cards, and whether governance is actually followed in deployments[7].

## 2) **MAP** — context and risk-scope controls that can be tested by scenario design and data lineage review

- **Validity** — check whether the intended use, task scope, and acceptance criteria are clearly defined so test cases match the real use case[7].
- **Reliability** — verify whether operating conditions, user populations, and environmental assumptions are documented so tests cover expected and edge conditions[7].
- **Robustness** — test whether known perturbations, prompt variations, and distribution shifts are anticipated in the mapped context of use[7].
- **Safety** — check whether hazardous misuse cases, unsafe outputs, and downstream harms are identified in the system context[7].
- **Security / resilience** — verify whether threat models include adversarial prompts, data poisoning, jailbreaks, and supply-chain dependencies[7].
- **Fairness / bias** — check whether stakeholder groups, vulnerable populations, and harmful disparity risks are identified in the context and data mapping[7].
- **What a tester would check** — use-case documentation, data provenance, prompt/input assumptions, user-stakeholder mapping, deployment constraints, and whether test suites reflect those mapped risks[7].

## 3) **MEASURE** — the most directly testable function for LLM/AI-app evaluation

- **Validity** — measure whether the model does what it is supposed to do on representative tasks and golden datasets[7].
- **Reliability** — check consistency across repeated runs, seeds, prompt paraphrases, sessions, and time[7].
- **Robustness** — evaluate performance under noisy inputs, adversarial prompts, context truncation, out-of-distribution inputs, and tool failures[7].
- **Safety** — test for harmful content generation, unsafe advice, policy violations, and refusal behavior on disallowed prompts[7].
- **Security / resilience** — run prompt-injection, jailbreak, exfiltration, and tool-abuse tests; check recovery under dependency failures and adversarial inputs[7].
- **Accountability / transparency** — verify logging, traceability of outputs to inputs, model/version attribution, and whether outputs can be audited after the fact[7].
- **Explainability / interpretability** — check whether the system exposes usable rationales, citations, or decision traces appropriate to the product’s risk level; also verify that explanation quality matches the model’s actual behavior[7].
- **Privacy** — test for memorization, sensitive-data leakage, training-data regurgitation, and prompt-based exposure of personal data[7].
- **Fairness / bias** — evaluate subgroup performance, disparity in error rates, toxic stereotyping, and whether outputs differ materially across demographic or proxy attributes[7].
- **What a tester would check** — test plans, metric definitions, acceptance thresholds, baseline comparisons, red-team results, monitoring outputs, and repeatability of findings[7].

## 4) **MANAGE** — mitigation and continuous-control tests

- **Validity** — check whether mitigations actually improve the target task, not just the metric, and whether rollback criteria are defined[7].
- **Reliability** — verify that patches, guardrails, and fallbacks reduce variance and instability without breaking core functionality[7].
- **Robustness** — test whether mitigations remain effective across attack variants and shifted inputs[7].
- **Safety** — confirm that unsafe outputs are blocked, escalated, or transformed into safe alternatives under realistic use[7].
- **Security / resilience** — test that containment, filtering, access control, and incident-response controls work under attack and failure conditions[7].
- **Accountability / transparency** — verify that risk decisions, mitigations, exceptions, and residual-risk acceptances are documented and reviewable[7].
- **Privacy** — check that privacy mitigations such as minimization, redaction, access restrictions, and retention controls are actually enforced[7].
- **Fairness / bias** — test whether bias mitigations reduce disparity without introducing new errors or regressions[7].
- **What a tester would check** — post-mitigation retests, control effectiveness evidence, monitoring triggers, escalation workflows, and residual-risk documentation[7].

## 5) **Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)** — generative-AI-specific test surfaces

NIST says the Generative AI Profile is intended to identify **unique risks posed by generative AI** and propose actions aligned with organizational goals and priorities[4].

- **Validity** — check whether generated outputs are on-task, grounded, and fit-for-purpose for the intended generative use case[4].
- **Reliability** — test output stability across repeated prompts, conversation state, and model versions[4].
- **Robustness** — evaluate susceptibility to prompt injection, jailbreaks, instruction conflicts, and context manipulation[4].
- **Safety** — test for harmful instructions, unsafe advice, disallowed content, and dangerous completions[4].
- **Security / resilience** — check resistance to exfiltration, indirect prompt injection, tool misuse, and malicious document/content inputs[4].
- **Accountability / transparency** — verify logging, provenance, disclosure of AI-generated content, and traceability for generated artifacts[4].
- **Explainability / interpretability** — check whether the system can provide useful provenance, citations, or rationale for generated answers when required by the use case[4].
- **Privacy** — test for leakage of personal data in outputs, training-data memorization, and unsafe retention of prompts or conversation history[4].
- **Fairness / bias** — measure stereotyped, exclusionary, or disparate outputs across demographic prompts and persona variations[4].
- **What a tester would check** — prompt suites, adversarial prompts, hallucination/grounding checks, output safety filters, provenance controls, and whether the application discloses generative-AI limitations appropriately[4].

## 6) Practical test mapping by characteristic

| Characteristic | Concrete LLM/AI-app test | NIST RMF function(s) | NIST publication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Validity** | Golden-set task accuracy, requirement conformance, grounded-answer checks | MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Reliability** | Repeatability across runs, prompts, sessions, and versions | MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7] |
| **Robustness** | Perturbation, jailbreak, OOD, and prompt-injection testing | MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Safety** | Harmful-content and unsafe-advice tests | GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Security / resilience** | Exfiltration, tool abuse, poisoning, and recovery tests | GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Accountability / transparency** | Logging, traceability, disclosure, auditability checks | GOVERN, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Explainability / interpretability** | Rationale/provenance usability tests | MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Privacy** | Memorization, leakage, redaction, and retention tests | GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |
| **Fairness / bias** | Subgroup disparity, stereotyping, and differential output tests | GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE | **NIST AI 100-1**[7]; **NIST AI 600-1**[4] |

If you want, I can turn this into a **test matrix with columns for control, test method, pass/fail criterion, evidence artifact, and automation candidate**.
